trajectory_summary: The call built genuine rapport through an unexpected bicycle-racing detour and landed a booked appointment, but the structural quality of that booking is fragile. Anne (co-title holder) was never included, the bill amount is a placeholder, and the button-up was missing multiple required elements. Bucky's warmth and patience are real assets; his protocol execution is the gap.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment for the following morning at 10AM, so a 'booked' outcome tag would be accurate. However, the booking is conditionally fragile: Anne is excluded, the bill is a placeholder, and no computer/no-driving/one-hour-block anchors were set. The closer walks in with less than a full setup.

primary_coaching_focus.why: Anne is on the title and was never included in the appointment. This is the single highest-risk structural gap on this call — the closer walks into a proposal with only one of two decision-makers present. Bucky captured the information correctly and then did nothing with it. This is a repeatable miss that will cost sits, not just this one appointment.

key_moments.quote: I had the idea that there would be some sort of someone would tell me what government help there is and, how much we're talking about.
key_moments.stewart_read: Banks is revealing an incentive-motivated customer — he filled out the form specifically because he expected to learn about government programs and financial structure. This is a depth signal on the emotional anchor question, not just a curiosity about cost. Bucky's response pivoted immediately to the bill-swap program pitch rather than dwelling on what specifically attracted Banks to the incentive angle. The emotional anchor — 'I want to do the right thing AND I want to understand the financial help available' — was never developed.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. It's called the bill swap program. And, actually, we replace your power bill with a lower and cheaper solar bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is execution of the required program_pitch beat (call_shape order 3). The one-liner bill-swap framing is structurally correct and directly responsive to Banks's question about financial structure. Where mild scope expansion begins is the follow-on: 'we cover the installation and the panels with no upfront cost' (02:24) — that's closer-tier detail that wasn't asked for. The core pitch line itself is not a problem.

key_moments.quote: Do you have a credit score over 670? I what is a 670? Oh, do you have a credit score over 670? Oh, no. I think it's less than that. Does, your wife have one over 670? No. Wait a minute. What do you a credit card? No credit score. Oh, boy. I don't I I've never done a credit score. I don't owe any money. I'm Okay. But so I think we have a pretty good credit score.
key_moments.stewart_read: Banks's self-assessment is a three-part contradiction: 'I think it's less than 670' → 'I've never done a credit score' → 'I think we have a pretty good credit score.' These three statements cannot all be true simultaneously. Bucky accepted the final optimistic statement and moved on without clarification. Per the Spencer operator note in qualifiers.credit_qualifier, stated credit is unreliable — a customer who has never checked their score cannot accurately self-report. Bucky's acceptance of 'pretty good credit score' as a pass is a potential DQ risk that the closer will inherit.

Anne is on the title. The codex is unambiguous: co-title holders must be included in the appointment. Bucky captured the information ('K.') and moved to the next verify question. Anne was never mentioned again. The appointment was booked for Banks alone at 22:55. This is a protocol violation, not a judgment call.
